speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is June 21, 1995. We're talking with Mr. Harry Toporek. This is Lincolnwood, Illinois, USA. Tape number 4. Mr. Toporek, you were telling us about Jaworzno. At what point did you leave Jaworzno?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there from 1943 until-- we worked in the coal mines-- until January 1945, January 18. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then they had orders that we should evacuate all the camps, and Jaworzno was part of that camp. And we start marching.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my brother, with my friend David Leder, and a lot-- what we were together from my town, some people. We always used to stick together, you know, with certain people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with anyone of another nationality? Was it just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I mean, we were with a lot of nationalities. Jaworzno was lots of nationalities. You got lots of Russian people. You had some Germans. You had Polish people. From all over Europe, you find people-- French people, French Jews and non-Jews, Belgium. You got people from all over the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was that march like? Tell me about that march.

speaker: interviewee
text: We start marching in the evening. And I remember the SS leader. He said-- he came to us and he said that, we are planning a long and a heavy march. Whoever is not capable of walking or feels not to go with us should remain in here. But everybody was afraid. They were afraid to take chances to remain in here because we know that the sick people-- what they're doing to the-- the sick people, the people that aren't-- aren't capable to work. So we all start marching out, and we start marching. The first day-- that-- well, if I'm not mistaken it was a Sunday. The first day, that wasn't too bad. By the second day, people start already-- they couldn't walk. And they start shooting left and right. You constantly could hear gunshots. People what couldn't walk were just pulled out from the line and shot to death. And we marched [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] an entire week. We slept sometime by a farmer. We used to sleep in a farmhouse or in-- or in the field in the [NON-ENGLISH]. And we were-- next day we have to continuously to march.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of a sudden, one day that was-- and I-- I think it was exactly eight days, in the morning a SS guy what worked in the-- an SS man that worked in the kitchen in Jaworzno, he used to came back with the motorcycle and he said, where do you want to go? You going to go to [? Oppeln, ?] a city in Germany? [? Oppeln ?] is by the-- is occupied by the Soviets. There is no use going there because the Soviets are there. So they start looking up the plans, the maps, and find out, and they told us to make a left turn through fields, and they start chasing us. And they start using machine guns, you know, machine guns, and they used to shoot them. Whoever fell, fell. And we had to run like cattle, and lots of people died. Lots of people were killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning, early in the morning, we got in in a camp, but the camp was also part of Auschwitz. They called it Blechhammer. Blechhammer, at that time, was in process of being liquidated. The people already had their portions, and they start marching. So the SS men told us whoever wants to remain should remain, and whoever feels like walking with them can walk with them. As far as we are concerned, it did not make any difference. Because if you're going to march, that's going to be the end. And let them do whatever-- it's-- they're-- going to happen, it's going to happen. We decided not to go anymore, because, you know, we have seen that we're not going to last long in marching.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put us in-- in Blechhammer. In Blechhammer, a couple hours later we find out that the SS were gone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The guards what used to watch the-- the camp, there is nobody. No Germans anymore. So we start going out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As we go out, we start looking actually for bread because we were hungry. We were like-- like wild wolves. I know lots of people used to go in and bring bread. And my brother went out there also, and somebody took away the bread. Because, you know, you got-- you find three bread, you got 200 people that want to grab it. So I went in. I-- I went out, and I went in the kitchen. I find the potatoes. That was beautiful potatoes, ready, but they haven't got the chance to distribute the potatoes for the people because they had to run away from that camp in Blechhammer. So I start putting in. I tighten my slacks, you know. I put a piece of wire, and I put in left and right that I should take the potatoes to my-- to give to my brother in the barrack. And out of nowhere, I hear that they start shooting out on the yard. I didn't know what happened. I take a look. I see some people what were in the kitchen were shot. So I open up-- I notice that there was a kitchen that was a-- a-- they used to make coffee, a big pot. They used to make coffee for-- for-- for several hundred people. What I did is I jumped in in that coffee pot, and I closed the door. And whoever was in the kitchen was shot, and then they start shooting the people outside. So the SS came back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What they did? They killed whoever they could. Thousands of people were shot during the hour. Maybe they took an hour, an hour and a half. And then they just disappeared. So when I-- the shooting stopped.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have seen from the-- looking out, I could see that they were burning. Barrack after barrack, they were burning, and they were staying with machine gun. Whoever would try to save his life, they were-- they were killed. Now, the next day, that was mountains of dead bodies. We had to put them together, because, you know, wherever you went they had to put them. Like you see sometime in this-- in-- in Bergen-Belsen, the mountains of body. That was in January 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the thing stopped, I walked out. And I went into the camp, and I have seen-- to the barrack, and I have seen my brother, and I told him what's happened. Lucky me that I used to have shoes from wood. If I wouldn't have wooden shoes, I couldn't stay in that part because the bottom was still hot. You know, that would burn me out.

speaker: interviewee
text: In any event, I-- I came back, and I-- we shared. We ate the potatoes. And then we went-- it's quiet. About 3 o'clock-- 3 or 4 o'clock in the afternoon-- they came back. The SS came back. They went on the-- the towers, the watch towers, and just start shooting again at us.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember I was with my brother. We just jumped, and we knocked the glass out from the barrack, and we jumped in in the barrack. At least we were protected. They cannot see any-- anybody, you know. They only shoot on people they could see, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it took about three, four hours, and it stopped again. I mean, they came back on their own. I don't know who-- who sent them there and what's [INAUDIBLE]. The next morning, that was quiet. We didn't hear anything. That was nobody there. No Russian, no Germans. We were not guarded by nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we tried to organize whatever we could. Everybody was making-- we were expecting that maybe the next day the-- the Russians going to come. We were there for about four days. The Russians didn't get in, and the Germans didn't come back. And we didn't know what's happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Out after four or five days, lots of people went out from that camp. They went to the forest. They were there for three, four days, and they came back because, you know, that they have seen lots of German military activities still around. They were not sure. We didn't know what's going to happen. when the Russians going to come. And one day, after three, four days being without anybody, in the morning, about 3 o'clock in the morning, regular police came in-- German police-- and they knocked at the windows and they said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Get ready, We're going to be marching away out of here. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Then they took us to a town. Yeah, before we went out, I figure should we hide or shouldn't we hide? And I tried to discuss this thing with-- with my friend. And before I turned around, I was already surrounded by the Germans. They said, out, out, that they-- that they had the machine guns ready. And they chased us out, and we start marching again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from that point on, the next day they turned over to the SS. The SS was afraid to come in. That was too close from the Russian front. And I find out later on that the next morning-- we left about 3 o'clock in the morning-- maybe three hours later the Russians came in. And we were in process of marching. And we still were marching on to the SS, and the shooting was going on just-- just as like it was before. Whoever couldn't work was killed. Then we marched [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from Blechhammer. I don't know how many days this took. We got into a camp, Gross Rosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you reached Gross Rosen, did you go through any cities?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went into lots of cities. I remember I went to a city that was Goldberg town. In Germany, Goldberg-- I mean, that-- it's, you know, very unusual. And we were not in-- they-- they tried to hide from the cities. They tried to go around places to eliminate the cities. But I remember we went to certain cities what I don't remember. But Goldberg I do remember. Beautiful German cities. I remember also [NON-ENGLISH] we used to go through, a city, and march. And we got into Gross Rosen. As we came in, transport was shipped out from Gross Rosen away. They gave us a half of bread and a piece of meat, and we were transported. We didn't know where. Some people they put in-- we all went in cattle cars, naturally. Some were open without a roof. Some were closed. When we got in-- took us about five or six days-- we got into Weimar. From Weimar-- this is part of-- of Buchenwald. When we came into Weimar, we couldn't walk anymore. We couldn't-- the trains couldn't get in because the Americans bombed the railroad, so we couldn't get in. And we went in marching in to Buchenwald. When we came to Buchenwald, my brother Sam was very sick. He got frozen legs. His feet was frozen, and he was in terrible pain. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he begged me that I should go on, that I should save myself. I said, no, this-- as long I will be able to go, you will be able to go. And I took him out in Buchenwald. He couldn't walk, so I carried him into the block. Waited three, four days to get in because everybody tried to get in. But after you get the [? bat, ?] then you got something to eat. But I didn't left Sam alone. We took him-- I took when he was sick. He couldn't walk. He just could not step on his feet. So I carried him on my shoulders, and I took him into block-- block 57 in Buchenwald. And he was there, and I was being. In the same block was a part like three bunks for-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was allocated for the sick people, and that was a doctor there. And he was there-- he was laying there sick that-- they couldn't do-- they didn't have any medical-- [NON-ENGLISH] they didn't have any medicine for it. And he was in pain, and I tried to go and tried to help him as much as he can-- as-- as-- as much as I can. And the doctor got a liking to me, and he said to me that I should help him, what would help. But people that complained that they don't feel good, they had to line up in lines. So I tried to-- to see that the-- that the people should stay in line. And he was the doctor-- he was a real doctor-- and he was decided who should go out-- who can go out on a roll call, and who cannot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the meantime, transports went away from Buchenwald to different camps. And one day-- I don't know how many days I was there-- I remember that I made arrangement that my brother should remain there in a special place, Sam, and he remained.

speaker: interviewee
text: A transport went away, and they came in. An SS man comes into the camp and he said that the-- he needs the doctor going to go with this in this transport, and the assistants will remain. So I became automatically a full doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And listen, I took it over, I mean, for the-- for the medication and for the health. But he didn't do anything, and I didn't do either. That was no medicine. There was no aspirin, no-- absolutely nothing. So, and I was doing his job. He gave me-- he-- the SS man told me that I should put on a band, a Red Cross band, and I became the block-- block doctor. So, listen, what can you do? I became a title like this in no time.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there for maybe a week or two. Other transports went away from Buchenwald to other camps where they needed help. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] An SS man comes in and takes me away and that I'm going to go with the transport as a doctor with this and this transport. I didn't know what to say to him. He gave me orders to go, so I have to go. So I went with a bunch of people. We went to a-- a place. They shipped us to a place by the Elbe not far from Magdeburg. They used to call it Schonebeck. Like Schonebeck, you got [INAUDIBLE] here, Schonebeck by Magdeburg. And I came in in that camp. And then [? came-- ?] from the city came for the health department, and they start asking-- you know, they didn't even know what kind of people that came, or they need any kind of medication, or they tried to help us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's lucky me, the SS man came out and he said to him, what are you talking with that Jew over there? I mean, what kind of business have you got? He said, I'm from the health department. I just want to find out [? that ?] he needs any medication. And he said, the Lagerfuhrer, he said, the SS man, we got anything what we need. We got our own. We got more than we need. So he had to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I became over there the doctor, and I was there. We worked in a factory. People worked in a factory, and I was at home. And I was-- I was the-- the-- the-- the head doctor of over the camp. That was more no more. We didn't have any medication. We didn't-- and lots of people died. It didn't matter it was a doctor or was a, you know, was a phony doctor. In any event, we were there until the Americans took over Magdeburg, and then an order came that we have to leave. [SIGHS] When that SS man came in, I have seen-- we were splitting up bread either for six or eight or whatever that was-- I have seen that one open up the mouth of another prisoner and he took out the piece of bread from his mouth. Stole his bread from the mouth. And everybody-- every-- everything was in a-- in a-- in a hurry. And people came to me, they begged me I should leave them in the hospital. I don't know what to do myself. I could have remained there. Believe it or not, maybe two, three hours later the American came in there, and I was on my march. They took me again. From that camp in Schonebeck, we start marching, and we got into Oranienburg. Oranienburg and Sachsenhausen, those are two sister camps, like Auschwitz and Birkenau. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember I was there on April the 20th I was in that camp. When we got in, the Lagerfuhrer used to tell us that, we know that you are from many camps evacuated, but you don't have to pray that the Russian should come on this terrain. We got orders from the Reichsfuhrer Himmler all the Jews should be killed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were used to it. We're accustomed to it, you know? That didn't make any effect to us. Whatever is going to happen is going to happen. We remained there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I had a dream. My mother come to me-- came to me in a dream and she said, don't worry. They are [? surrendered. ?] They're going to-- you're going to march out from here. This is not the end. You know, a funny dream. This is the second time what I had dreamt that my mother came and she said that, don't worry about. You-- they are [? surrendered. ?] They're going to take you out. Don't worry that you will get killed. We start marching. And we start marching [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for days and days and days. I don't know how long. I remember-- in that process of march, I remember that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in march I find out also that President Roosevelt died, and they were celebrating his death. I know that he died-- that was the 12th of April, 1945, if I'm not mistaken. I think so. This I do remember, you see, that they were celebrating now actually the-- that the war going to go on their side. And we start marching. We didn't know where we're going, how we're going, what we're going. And the shooting was going on the same day. By the end, I didn't know where the Russians are, where the Germans are, where the American. I know that the Germans are watching us. And we used to sleep in the forest. And one time-- that was the 8th of May-- I mean, now I know, at that time, I didn't know-- they released all the prisoners, and they gave them packages from the Red Cross. But the Jewish prisoners they kept separate, and they let everybody go.

speaker: interviewer
text: After that, they took us into a farm-- to a farmhouse. They put us in in a barn. I mean, that was something unusual, because we used to sleep in the forest, you know? They used to watch us. They didn't care if the rain or shine. And I could hear that one SS man said to the other that, when it-- it's going to get dark, we're going to put-- put them in fire. [SPEAKING GERMAN], when it's going to get dark, then we're going to put everybody in flame. And they were watching us, you know? That was a big barn. There was-- I don't know how many-- hundreds of people were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told this thing to one of my friend what I was marching with, Sholek Bramberg. He came also from a town near Krakow. And I said, we got nothing to lose. Now is the last chance. Let's run.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we opened up two boards, you know, we loosen two boards. And when the SS men went around-- because there were only two are watching us-- so we run into the forest, and from the forest we run in to a farmhouse.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were trying to shoot at us, but nobody got hurt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went on the attic to the farm, and we could see a couple hours later they did set the barn on fire. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was May the 9th. And I also have seen that they took prisoners, three Russian prisoners. They were in the uniform and, naturally, without the arms. They put them in in a tank, an abandoned tank, and they put grenades in it. It exploded, the tank with the-- with the Russians. But our people were dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was only survived with this other friend, what we were in the attic, what we escaped. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next morning, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Russians came in. I mean, the morning when the Russians came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Naturally they insisted we should take all the bodies-- the dead bodies from the burn out-- we should give them a [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, we should bury them. Naturally we couldn't do it. We were only two people. And so they organized people from the village. And we had-- had to line them up while they took pictures. He didn't-- he did told me that I should go in in the picture, but I said, I don't want to be with dead people. You know, that was a frightening thing to stay next to dead people. He said I should say there. I could speak Russian, you know. I could speak because I was with Russian people. And he took those pictures, and those are the pictures what I show you. None-- there isn't one soul after 50 years what could say that he was liberated with Harry Toporek. And I was in so many camps and with so many hundreds of thousands of people, and that-- it was the day that I was liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were liberated with your friend. Again, his name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sholek Bramberg. And the next day, we said that we're going to go-- we're going to make something to eat. So I went into a farmer. I remember he made a fire. And he said, the next morning, he's going to go for water, and I-- I'm going to make the fire, vice versa. In the meantime, I have seen the German soldiers had food there, and I was eating their food. And their food was-- naturally that was nourishing food. That was meat and that kind of stuff. I got sick instantly on the spot. I couldn't eat anymore. And I brought some barley from the farmers. So he was cooking it, and he ate as much as he could. We were hungry for-- for weeks and weeks and weeks. The next morning, he was dead. I tried to wake him up in the morning, and I could see that he is-- did not respond. He was dead. Only I have seen water just coming out that looks like from the barley. Either they were not cooked, or he overate it. Regardless what the reason was, but he-- I remain alone. And that was the day, actually, I was liberated, that I was-- was a free person.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your emotions like the day you were liberated? Did you-- was it overwhelming? Did you realize that that was really it? That was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't matter to me. No, absolutely nothing. Didn't celebrate. Didn't care for nothing. I find-- later on, I find Russian prisoners, whatever released. Because the day before they released all the non-Jews-- that I was with them in-- in-- in the coal mines in Jaworzno. And they said that we'll-- let's-- let's start looking for horses, you know, and we're going to look for the SS what run away. Maybe they're around the vicinity. Because they were in the vicinity. Go-- go look a-- a needle in a hay-- in a-- in a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got in on a horse, and I-- I could not maneuver. That was a military horse. The Russian people, they were farmers. They were accustomed to horses. Then I said, let's-- let's-- I went on on a horse. The horse was going sideways, and I didn't know how to control it. I almost--- I fell off the horse. I said, I-- I survived the war. Now I'm going to get killed by the-- by a horse.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, we're going to go organize [? far out-- ?] that we-- bicycles. Because the Germans used to run away from the-- from the Russians, run to the American side. They knew where the Americans are. So I went in there with the prisoners what I was in the camp. And I went to-- I have seen there was a couple, an elderly couple, with a daughter with a child. And I try-- I have seen a bike. So I went on the-- on the [? wagon. ?] And I said, I'm going to take the-- the-- the bike away. So the woman said, it-- it's not so [? well ?] that you're going to take the bike away from me. I'm not going to let you. In German, it sounds different. It didn't go so far, I mean, that you're going to take the-- I'm-- I'm not going to allow it. And they-- the Russian people-- did not-- and the Russian, the-- the prisoner didn't understand what she was saying. So she asked me-- they asked me, what did she say, the-- the German woman? Said, Said, she doesn't want to give me the bike. So they took-- they had bayonet-- they took a baby-- she had a little baby. You know, they killed that baby. I mean, you know, I was so scared. This I do remember. I went down from that [INAUDIBLE], and I was shaking. I mean, why-- why did they have to kill that baby? Now they're doing the same thing they did to us, but it was for Russians. And I didn't want to stay with those people anymore. You know, whoever kills babies going-- you don't know what he's going to do with me the next day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I completely remained alone. I didn't have with anybody celebrate. It was nothing to-- to cry or to laugh. I know that I was sick. I was very undernourished. And that was the day, actually, I was free. I was on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
